perturbation.original_text: This Amendment shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Japan.
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of California, United States.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Japan to California introduces potential conflicts. California law has specific requirements for various business agreements, and this blanket change may invalidate clauses that are valid under Japanese law but not compliant with California regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 201 dictates when final wages are due to employees who are discharged. If the JV agreement involves employees in California and their employment is terminated as a result of the agreement, changing the governing law to California would subject the agreement to this statute. The original agreement may have different or no provisions regarding final wage payments, thus creating a contradiction.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 201. (a) If an employer discharges an employee, the wages earned and unpaid at the time of discharge are due and payable immediately. An employer who lays off a group of employees by reason of the termination of seasonal employment in the curing, canning, or drying of any variety of perishable fruit, fish, or vegetables, shall be deemed to have made immediate payment when the wages of said employees are paid within a reasonable time as necessary for computation and payment thereof; provided, however, that the reasonable time shall not exceed 72 hours, and further provided that payment shall be made by mail to any employee who so requests and designates a mailing address therefor. (b) Notwithstanding any other law, the state employer shall be deemed to have made an immediate payment of wages under this section for any unused or accumulated vacation, annual leave, holiday leave, or time off to which the emp
perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Japan to California introduces a contradiction because California law, specifically Labor Code § 201, dictates when final wages are due to employees who are discharged. If the JV agreement involves employees in California and their employment is terminated as a result of the agreement, changing the governing law to California would subject the agreement to this statute. The original agreement may have different or no provisions regarding final wage payments, potentially creating a scenario where the agreement is non-compliant with California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states: 'If an employer discharges an employee, the wages earned and unpaid at the time of discharge are due and payable immediately'. Therefore, the change introduces a direct contradiction to California law if the agreement doesn't adhere to this immediate payment requirement upon discharge.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding Article 1.1, Article 1.2 and Article 1.3 above, the termination of the JV Agreement with respect to VNBJ, VNBZ or the Veoneer Parties shall not release any Veoneer Party or any Nissin Party from liability for the breach of any of its representations, warranties, covenants or agreements set forth in the JV Agreement that arise prior to the VNBJ Closing or the VNBZ Closing, as applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding Article 1.1, Article 1.2 and Article 1.3 above, the termination of the JV Agreement with respect to VNBJ, VNBZ or the Veoneer Parties shall not release any Veoneer Party or any Nissin Party from liability for the breach of any of its representations, warranties, covenants or agreements set forth in the JV Agreement that arise prior to 30 days after the VNBJ Closing or the VNBZ Closing, as applicable.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the liability for breaches to only those arising within 30 days of the closing could conflict with statutes of limitations for breach of contract, which are often longer than 30 days, thus making the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 establishes a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. By limiting liability to breaches arising within 30 days of the closing, the modified text directly contradicts the four-year statute of limitations, rendering the limitation likely unenforceable in California.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an
perturbation.justification: The modified text limits liability to breaches occurring within 30 days of the closing, while California Code of Civil Procedure § 337, as evidenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1, establishes a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. This creates a contradiction because the contractual limitation significantly shortens the legally mandated period for bringing a breach of contract claim, making the contractual limitation likely unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Article 4.Effectiveness This Amendment shall only become effective upon the VNBJ Closing with respect to Article 1.1 and the VNBZ Closing with respect to Article 1.2, and shall terminate without any force or effect in the event that the VNBJ SPA and the VNBZ SPA are terminated in accordance with the terms thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 4.Effectiveness This Amendment shall only become effective 90 days after the VNBJ Closing with respect to Article 1.1 and the VNBZ Closing with respect to Article 1.2, and shall terminate without any force or effect regardless of whether the VNBJ SPA and the VNBZ SPA are terminated.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the effectiveness of the amendment by 90 days after closing, regardless of the original agreements' terminations, could create issues with fulfilling contractual obligations immediately upon closing, potentially conflicting with securities regulations related to timely disclosure and execution of material agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 13a-11 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 requires companies to file a current report (Form 8-K) disclosing material events, which includes entry into or termination of a material definitive agreement, typically within four business days of the event. By delaying the effectiveness of the amendment by 90 days and stating the agreement will terminate regardless of the VNBJ/VNBZ SPA termination (which are likely to be considered material agreements), this contradicts the requirement for immediate disclosure and timely execution of material agreements per securities law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13a-11 - Current reports on Form 8-K (§ 249.308 of this chapter). | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13a-11 - Current reports on Form 8-K (§ 249.308 of this chapter). CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13a-11 Current reports on Form 8-K (§ 249.308 of this chapter). (a) Except as provided in paragraph (b) of this section , every registrant subject to § 240.13a-1 shall file a current report on Form 8-K within the period specified in that form unless substantially the same information as that required by Form 8-K has been previously reported by the registrant . (b) This section shall not apply to foreign governments, foreign private issuers required to make reports on Form 6-K ( § 249.306 of this chapter) pursuant to § 240.13a-16, issuers of American Depositary Receipts for securities of any foreign issuer , or investment companies required to file reports pursuant to § 270.30a-1 of this chapter under the Investment Company Act of 1940 , except where such an investment company is required to file notice of a blackout period pursuant to § 245.104 of this chapter. (c) No failure to file a report on Form 8-K that is required solely pursuant to Item 1.01, 1.02, 1.05, 2.03, 2.04, 2.05, 2.06, 4.02(a), 5.02(e), or 6.03 of Form 8-K shall be deemed to be a violation of 15 U.S.C. 78j(b) and § 240.10b-5. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 90-day delay in the amendment's effectiveness and terminates it regardless of the SPA's termination. This contradicts the requirement for immediate disclosure of material events. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.13a-11, states that registrants must file a current report on Form 8-K within the period specified in that form, which implies a timely disclosure. The 90-day delay violates this requirement, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable due to non-compliance with securities regulations, thus creating a contradiction.
